reasoning: Q1 is embarrassingly parallel with 98.8% filter selectivity on 60M rows. With 64 cores and columnar storage, near-linear speedup is achievable through aggressive parallelism and SIMD vectorization. GenDB already beats DuckDB (48ms vs 87ms), so initial implementation should maintain this advantage.
focus_areas: Parallel scan with 64 threads using morsel-driven parallelism (~10K-50K row morsels per thread)
SIMD-accelerated filtering on l_shipdate using AVX512 for date comparisons
SIMD-accelerated arithmetic for expressions: l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount) * (1 + l_tax)
Partial aggregation: each thread maintains local hash table for 4 groups, then merge globally
Zone map exploitation: use shipdate_zonemap to skip blocks outside date range (though 98.8% selectivity limits gains)
Columnar access: only load 7 columns needed (l_shipdate, l_quantity, l_extendedprice, l_discount, l_tax, l_returnflag, l_linestatus)
Cache-aware morsel sizing: target L3_cache/64 threads ≈ ~11K rows to fit in cache
Pre-sized hash tables: only 4 groups (returnflag × linestatus), pre-allocate to avoid rehashing
strategy_notes: Q1 is a textbook parallel scan-filter-aggregate. The 98.8% filter selectivity means arithmetic and aggregation dominate, not filtering. Prioritize: (1) Maximum parallelism across 64 cores with morsel-driven execution, (2) SIMD for arithmetic expressions (3 multiplications per row), (3) Partial aggregation to minimize contention (each thread aggregates locally, then merge 64 → 1). The extremely low group count (4) makes hash table overhead negligible. Columnar storage already provides excellent cache locality. Expected performance: near-linear speedup (64 cores ≈ 60x), targeting <10ms if perfectly optimized, but 48ms baseline is already competitive. Focus on maintaining code simplicity while maximizing parallelism — avoid premature complexity.
notes: Iteration 0: No prior evaluation. GenDB baseline (48ms) already beats DuckDB (87ms). Initial code should be aggressive with parallelism and SIMD to maintain this lead. The Query Optimizer should generate clean, morsel-driven parallel code with AVX512 intrinsics for arithmetic. Avoid over-engineering — the query is simple and benefits most from brute-force parallelism.
